title: LLM-Powered Multimodal and Blockchain-Integrated Framework for Enhanced Certificate Authenticity Verification
authors: Anuj Razdan, Akhil Sharma, Rajiv Kr Berwer, Riya, Monika, Ghanshyam Yadav
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Innovation for Sustainability (EmergIN)
abstract: The proliferation of digital certificates across educational and professional domains has intensified the need for robust authenticity verification mechanisms. Contemporary forgery techniques exploit advanced artificial intelligence and sophisticated image manipulation tools, rendering traditional verification approaches inadequate. This research presents an innovative framework that synergistically combines Large Language Models (LLMs) with blockchain technology to establish a dual-layer trust architecture for certificate verification. Our approach leverages transformer-based language models for comprehensive multimodal analysis—simultaneously processing textual content and visual elements without requiring separate optical character recognition or convolutional neural network modules. The integration of blockchain technology through smart contracts and Interplanetary File System (IPFS) storage creates an immutable verification infrastructure that complements the LLM’s analytical capabilities. Experimental validation demonstrates exceptional performance metrics: 96.7% accuracy in authenticity detection, 95.3% precision, and 94.8% recall, while maintaining blockchain verification latency under 2.1 seconds. The proposed dual-layer trust mechanism significantly outperforms conventional single-modal approaches and existing blockchain-only solutions, establishing a new paradigm for tamper-proof certificate verification systems.

title: LAPPI: Interactive Optimization with LLM-Assisted Preference-Based Problem Instantiation
authors: So Kuroki, Manami Nakagawa, Shigeo Yoshida, Yuki Koyama, Kozuno Tadashi
abstract: Many real-world tasks, such as trip planning or meal planning, can be formulated as combinatorial optimization problems. However, using optimization solvers is difficult for end users because it requires problem instantiation: defining candidate items, assigning preference scores, and specifying constraints. We introduce LAPPI (LLM-Assisted Preference-based Problem Instantiation), an interactive approach that uses large language models (LLMs) to support users in this instantiation process. Through natural language conversations, the system helps users transform vague preferences into well-defined optimization problems. These instantiated problems are then passed to existing optimization solvers to generate solutions. In a user study on trip planning, our method successfully captured user preferences and generated feasible plans that outperformed both conventional and prompt-engineering approaches. We further demonstrate LAPPI’s versatility by adapting it to an additional use case.

title: Interactive AI Training Agent for Tomato Farmers: A GenAI and LLM driven Approach to Agriculture 4.0
publish: 2025 International Conference On Emerging Computation and Information Technologies (ICECIT)
abstract: Agriculture in developing regions continues to face challenges of limited access to timely, localized, and expert guidance. Traditional advisory systems often lack scalability, personalization, and contextual relevance. Advances in Artificial Intelligence (AI), particularly Generative AI (GenAI), Large Language Models (LLMs), and autonomous agents, provide opportunities for more adaptive solutions. This paper proposes an Interactive Training Agent for Tomato Farmers that combines multimodal interfaces, retrieval-augmented generation, and AI-driven modules for disease detection, soil analytics, and predictive insights. It is multimodal voice, text and image and edge deployed, which makes it reliable in rural areas and achieved 98% detection accuracy on different tomato leaf diseases. The framework belongs to Agriculture 4.0 that is supposed to improve the decision-making process of farmers, reduce crop losses, and produce tomatoes sustainably.

title: Unified Food Tracking and Smart Assistance: Enhancing Personalized Nutrition with LLM Integration
authors: Abhijeet Mohapatra, Aditya Kumar Jha, Parthsarthi Dutt, Kavita Jaiswal
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Intelligent Network Computing (ICDSINC)
abstract: A centralized system that along with monitoring your food consumption, also provides you with intelligent consultations and advice is a crucial need in the times of digital medicine. This paper reflects the idea and execution of a complete end-to-end and seamless food diary and chatbot system where users can track their multiple nutrient consumption by inculcating to it their meals, and interact with a smart chatbot which can use these information and with the help of AI, provide appropriate advice. This application is developed with a modern web development stack and is very dynamic, begineer-friendly and scalable for self nutritional tracking. Including Biomistral's large language model (LLM) also optimizes user experience and engage context-full conversations. The paper addresses the system design, implementation, evaluation and servey of relevant works and technologies in this field.

title: The Dual Role of Artificial Intelligence and LLM in Cybersecurity
authors: Leandros Maglaras, Vassilis C. Gerogiannis, Mohamed Amine Ferrag, Alexios Lekidis, Abderrahmane Lakas, Naghmeh Moradpoor
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence Security and Governance (ICAISG)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Large Language Models (LLMs) are transforming the cybersecurity landscape, taking a dual role as both enablers of defense. This paper explores this duality by distinguishing between AI for Security, where intelligent models enhance threat analysis, intrusion detection, and automated mitigation, and Security for AI, which focuses on securing models themselves against adversarial manipulation, data leakage, and prompt-based exploitation. To empirically evaluate the capabilities of modern LLMs in cybersecurity, we conducted extensive experiments using the newly developed CyberMetric dataset, which assesses cybersecurity knowledge across multiple domains such as cryptography, network defense, and software vulnerabilities. Our evaluation of twenty state-of-the-art LLMs—including GPT-5 Chat, Grok 4 Fast, Qwen3 235B, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and DeepSeek V3.2—demonstrates that the 2025 generation of models achieves near-human performance, with GPT-5 Chat reaching 98.75% accuracy and several open-source models performing within a 2-3% margin. These results highlight the growing maturity of open LLM ecosystems for cybersecurity applications and confirm that modern LLMs are becoming reliable instruments for cyber defense, capable of supporting automated threat analysis, vulnerability assessment, and compliance verification, while also emphasizing the need to ensure their safety, transparency, and resilience against misuse.

title: LLM-Empowered Decision-Focused Learning for the Operation of Local Energy Communities
authors: Yangze Zhou, Yu Zuo, Daniel Kirschen, Yi Wang
abstract: Energy forecasting and dispatch are two essential procedures of local energy community (LEC) operations. In practice, these two procedures are often modeled and solved independently, leading to misaligned objectives, i.e., minimizing forecasting errors and costs. Recently, decision-focused learning (DFL) has been proposed to integrate these two procedures, but most existing works can merely be applied to a specific forecasting model or optimization problem. These methods are also technically complex for the operators to understand and implement. Therefore, this work proposes a large language model (LLM)-empowered DFL framework. Three LLM-based agents collaborate to fine-tune forecasts: one performs few-shot learning, one analyzes strategies, and one handles reflection. The case studies are conducted across four settings, two of which are challenging for existing methods, and tested with LLMs from three different providers. The results show that our method can achieve lower costs than existing approaches and requires no additional model training.

title: Enhancing Outpatient Care Using an LLM-Based Model for Clinical Ward Disposition
authors: Nancy Jane Y, Kingslin Issac R, Vinishaa K R, Vidhya Bharathi S, Ramya D B
publish: 2026 International Conference on Computing, Communication, Control and Cyber-Physical Systems (I5CPS)
abstract: Enhancing outpatient care in the Emergency Department (ED) requires optimized patient flow to reduce waiting times and improve hospital resource utilization. This work presents a Clinical Ward Disposition (CWD) system designed to optimize patient flow and support admission decisions following initial nursing assessments. The proposed system uses a hybrid CNN–BiLSTM model for optical character recognition (OCR) to digitize handwritten admission notes, achieving an accuracy of 86.57%. The extracted clinical information is analyzed using ClinicalBERT and XLNet models to determine the most appropriate ward for patient admission, achieving a disposition accuracy of 90.44%. The proposed system improves patient care by automating note digitization, extracting clinical data, and supporting efficient ward disposition decisions.

title: NeuroSecure-RISC: LLM-Guided ISA Co-Design and Reinforcement Learning Fuzzing for Secure and Efficient Spiking Neural Network Acceleration on RISC-V
authors: C. Sonika, P Sudha, T. Swarna Latha, Vakati Kishore, K. Kannaiah
publish: 2025 International Conference on Data, Energy and Communication Networks (DECoN)
abstract: The development of neuromorphic computing and the RISC-V-spiking neural network (SNN) accelerators has become an important source of energy-efficient edge intelligence. Recent designs, including SpikeStream and FeNN, achieved up to 4.4x speedup and 3.5x energy due to vectorized sparse processing and RV-SCNN and RISCVuzz achieved 90 % of inference-cycle improvement and architecture-level fuzzing to improve hardware reliability. At the same time, fuzzing-based designs such as MABFuzz and ChatFuzz demonstrated higher vulnerability detection by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$308 x$</tex> and 75 coverage of 52 min and guaranteed reliable neuromorphic execution. Such fundamental systems as Loihi, SpiNNaker succeeded in tens-to-hundreds-fold energy reductions, establishing benchmarks to on-chip learning. Even with such innovations, it is still very difficult to combine fault detection, side-channel resistance, and adaptive energy control into single unified RISC-V systems. The paper suggests a Hybrid Adaptive Neuromorphic-RISC-V (HAN-RV) architecture which is a combination of spike-based workload profiling, dynamic voltage scaling, and bandit-based instruction fuzzing. Initial analysis shows that there is a possibility of 6 times throughput increase and power reduction of up to 80 percent over the state-of-the-art vectorized SNN cores. HAN-RV bridges the efficiency to neuromorphic inference and the security to verified and secure fuzzing strategies through the combination of learning-based optimization and the verified secure fuzzing strategies to offer a scalable roadmap of the next generation intelligent edge system.

title: KDG-Rec: Enhanced Dual-GNN Programming Exercise Recommendation via LLM-Powered Knowledge Annotation and Preference-Decoupling
authors: Bangqi Li, Qing Sun, Tianyi Zhu, Ji Wu, Li Jing, Wenge Rong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: The rapid expansion of online programming exercise platforms has brought abundant learning resources for programming education, but also presents challenges for personalized exercise recommendation due to missing or imprecise knowledge annotations and the sparsity of learner-exercise interaction records, which together lead to reduced accuracy and a lack of explainability in the recommendation results. Existing natural language processing and large language models (LLM)-based annotation methods struggle to capture implicit knowledge and often generate redundant or inconsistent results. Moreover, mainstream recommendation systems are ineffective at handling the severe sparsity of interaction sequences in programming exercise datasets, and typically model student preferences in a single dimension—overlooking key educational factors such as knowledge gaps, difficulty tolerance, and preferred learning rhythm. To address these challenges, we propose KDG-Rec, a novel framework that first introduces AgentCo-KAS, a multiagent LLM-based collaborative annotation method with role-specific fine-tuning and cross-agent verification for high-precision, fine-grained knowledge annotation. Building on these enriched annotations, we develop a disentangled graph neural network model that constructs dual exercise-interaction graphs to effectively capture learning patterns at multiple granularities in interaction sequences and explicitly decouples student preferences into four interpretable dimensions for adaptive fusion. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that KDG-Rec outperforms nine state-of-the-art methods across multiple metrics, significantly advancing personalized programming exercise recommendation.

title: Hybrid Blockchain Model for Identity Verification and Travel Security in Global Tourism Industry
publish: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Intelligent Network Computing (ICDSINC)
abstract: Identity verification and travel security are essential issues in the travel and tourism industry, and borders that are directly related to the safety, comfort, and privacy of individuals. Documents such as passports, visas, and national IDs can be forged or manipulated, which can lead to threats to national security, terrorist infiltration, and transnational criminal activities. Currently, the above problems cannot be solved with conventional systems. Thus, the purpose of this research is to develop a hybrid blockchain model that enhances travel protection by increasing the transparency of individual identity information and their travel activities. The proposed model utilizes the concept of a hybrid blockchain to strengthen the security of personal identities and their associated travel activities. A hybrid blockchain is a combination of a private blockchain and a public blockchain. The research employs a qualitative approach, utilizing a literature review to identify problems and alternative solutions related to blockchain technology. The Hyperledger simulation results indicate that a private blockchain network will store crucial information, such as individual identity and travel details. The simulation results also show that each transaction must go through a peer-to-peer validation process before being stored on the blockchain network.

title: JADE: Joint Architecture-Dataflow Exploration for LLM Inference on Heterogeneous In- and Near-memory Computing Systems
authors: Yimin Wang, Zhen Wu, Yue Jiet Chong, Xuanyao Fong
publish: IEEE Journal on Emerging and Selected Topics in Circuits and Systems
abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) inference challenges memory/computing organization and dataflow optimization on traditional hardware stacks due to its various attention mechanisms and autoregressive structure. In-memory computing (IMC) and near-memory computing (NMC) architectures have emerged as potential solutions to reduce the memory burden in LLMs. However, efficient LLM orchestration on IMC/NMC, as well as dedicated IMC/NMC architecture optimization, are interrelated, encompassing a hardware-software co-design space of a myriad of parameters, which still remains largely underexplored. This work provides a joint architecture-dataflow exploration (JADE) framework that covers both architectural factors on computing/memory/communication, and algorithmic factors on LLM dataflow. We identify key configurations in four categories: computing and memory organization, interconnection topology, matrix multiplication (MM) unrolling and mapping, and attention mechanism. A unified MM representation enables flexible unrolling and mapping on various architectural organizations. Dataflow is routed based on the collective communication patterns inherent in the distributed LLMs and the features of common topologies. Finally, case studies show the design tradeoffs between key design options and provide insights into both homogeneous and heterogeneous IMC/NMC design for LLMs.

title: Automated PI Parameter Optimization for LLRF Systems: A Comparative Study of LLM-guided parameter tuning and Bayesian Optimization Approaches
authors: Lijuan Yang, Yilin Miao, Shihui Wei, Zhenglong Zhu, Zhen Gao, Zhaojie Chen, Qizhen Hou, Jiayi Peng, Jinying Ma, Chengye Xu, Xinghao Ding, Guirong Huang, Liepeng Sun, Rihua Zeng, Zhijun Wang, Feng Qiu, Yuan He
abstract: This paper presents a comparative study of LLM-guided parameter tuning and Bayesian Optimization (BO) for automated PI parameter adjustment in Low Level Radio Frequency (LLRF) systems. Both methods demonstrate effective performance across continuous wave and pulsed operation modes, addressing the challenges of traditional manual tuning in superconducting accelerator facilities. Multi-cavity validation experiments were conducted on both operation modes. For continuous wave 2-parameter optimization, both methods converge to optimal regions within 15 iterations. For pulsed 6-parameter optimization involving coupled amplitude-phase control loops, both methods achieve satisfactory results in around 20 iterations, meeting stability requirements for beam operation. Comparative analysis reveals distinct application advantages: BO is better suited for large-scale batch optimization scenarios, particularly excelling in multi-cavity parallel commissioning and high-dimensional parameter space exploration due to its systematic probabilistic framework. The LLM-guided approach is more appropriate for online optimization scenarios with strict operational constraints, where its conservative optimization strategy and traceable reasoning process make it ideal for parameter fine-tuning during operations. The study provides practical method selection guidelines for intelligent operation of large-scale accelerator facilities.
